question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of per-IP or per-user rate limiting in rateLimitBucket (it is a single global bucket) to monopolize the entire requestsPerSecond budget, starving other users and effectively modifying the transaction fee throughput available to legitimate users?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the global shared gasLimitBucket (single instance for all users) to monopolize the entire gasPerSecond budget by sending a single request with gas equal to the full gasPerSecond capacity, blocking all other users from executing any gas-consuming operations for the entire second?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SynchronizationStrategy.SYNCHRONIZED on gasLimitBucket to create a thundering herd by sending many concurrent requests, causing all threads to queue on the synchronized block and creating a denial-of-service that modifies the effective transaction processing rate outside design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that scaleGas() returns 0 for any gas value <= GAS_SCALE_FACTOR (10000) to send a stream of requests with gas=10000, each consuming 0 gas tokens, effectively making the gasLimitBucket irrelevant and allowing unlimited gas-priced operations outside the design parameters?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket uses refillGreedy (tokens added as fast as possible) rather than refillIntervally (tokens added at fixed intervals), allowing the attacker to time requests to catch the bucket mid-refill and consume tokens faster than the intended 1-second rate, modifying the effective fee rate?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the restore() path in ContractController where throttleManager.restore(request.getGas()) is called with the raw (unscaled) gas value on InvalidParametersException, while throttle() consumed scaleGas(request.getGas()) tokens, to verify whether the restore correctly re-scales and whether any precision mismatch allows net token gain?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with gas=10000 (scaleGas=0), trigger an InvalidParametersException, and observe that restore(10000) calls scaleGas(10000) which returns 0, meaning no tokens are restored — but since none were consumed, this is a no-op — yet the rate limit token was consumed, creating an asymmetry that depletes the rate limit bucket without any gas budget impact?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the gasLimitBucket capacity is set to getGasPerSecond() (scaled value) but bucket4j's addTokens() can add tokens beyond capacity up to Long.MAX_VALUE in some versions, by triggering many concurrent restore() calls to inflate the bucket beyond its design capacity?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the minimum gasPerSecond constraint of 21_000 which after scaleGas() becomes only 2 tokens (21000/10000=2), meaning the gasLimitBucket has a capacity of only 2 tokens in minimum configuration, allowing a single request with gas=20000 to consume both tokens and completely block all other users for 1 second?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket has no synchronization strategy specified (defaults to lock-free CAS in bucket4j) while gasLimitBucket uses SYNCHRONIZED, creating a TOCTOU window between the two tryConsume() calls in throttle() where concurrent threads can both pass rateLimitBucket but only one passes gasLimitBucket, wasting rate limit tokens?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the gasLimitBucket refill period is exactly Duration.ofSeconds(1) with no jitter, allowing precise timing attacks where the attacker synchronizes requests to the refill boundary to consistently achieve maximum burst throughput and modify the effective gas fee rate?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that scaleGas() uses integer floor division (Math.floorDiv) to send gas values that are just below the next multiple of GAS_SCALE_FACTOR, systematically underpaying the gas throttle cost by up to GAS_SCALE_FACTOR-1 gas units per request, cumulatively consuming more gas than the design parameters allow?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the restoreGasToBucket() logic where gasLimitToRestoreBaseline uses float arithmetic (gasLimit * gasLimitRefundPercent / 100f) to cause floating-point rounding that results in restoring more tokens than were consumed, gradually inflating the gasLimitBucket beyond its design capacity?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user send a request with gas=15000000 (maxGasLimit), have it throttled by gasLimitBucket, then immediately send gas=10000 requests that consume 0 tokens, exploiting the scaleGas() zero-return to bypass the gas throttle while the bucket is depleted, effectively getting free execution during the throttle window?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Modification of transaction fees outside of design parameters'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the single global rateLimitBucket shared across all users to perform a rate-limit exhaustion attack by sending requestsPerSecond requests per second, consuming the entire global budget and preventing any other user from making requests, effectively modifying the transaction fee availability outside design parameters?
